methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $23M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
QuiX Quantum is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised $23M to date. The sector comprises 11 total comparables—3 publicly traded (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion—though the narrow peer set at identical stage and valuation tier is sparse.

The analysis applies stage-progression methodology, which is applicable given the company's defined funding milestone. Using total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A to subsequent-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $46M. A peer-median approach is not applicable due to insufficient granular comparables at the same stage and similar valuation tier; the broader quantum computing sector contains only one adjacent-stage peer and zero same-stage datapoints meeting filter criteria, falling short of the three-point minimum for reliable median analysis.

The triangulated range is $39M to $53M, with a median of $46M, derived from the single applicable method. Key uncertainties stem from the limited comparative dataset in quantum computing—a nascent sector with uneven funding and exit history—and the reliance on stage-progression multipliers which assume market conditions consistent with broader venture norms. Refinement of this range would benefit from additional private market transactions or clarified peer-stage distributions in the quantum subsector.
